subsections.title: Thread Fundamentals
subsections.keyRule: Thread = independent execution path. Java Thread has 6 states: NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED. Creating threads is expensive — use thread pools (ExecutorService) instead of raw Thread creation.

terms.what: A thread is an independent path of execution inside a JVM process. Java starts with one main thread. You create more for parallel work.
terms.why: Parallel threads can do multiple things simultaneously — serve multiple users, do background processing while handling requests.
terms.how: Three ways: 1) extend Thread + override run(). 2) implement Runnable + pass to new Thread(). 3) implement Callable + use ExecutorService (best). Raw Thread creation is expensive — use pools.
terms.where: Background jobs, parallel computations, async I/O. In modern Spring apps, most threading is handled by the framework (Tomcat threads, @Async, @Scheduled).
terms.when: Use ExecutorService for managed thread pools. Only create raw Thread for very specific low-level needs. Use virtual threads (Java 21) for high-concurrency I/O.
terms.issue: Creating a new Thread for every task creates and destroys threads constantly — heavy OS resource overhead.
terms.fix: ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10); pool.submit(task); — reuses threads. Always shutdown the pool when done.

star.s: App creates new Thread per HTTP request — crashes under load.
star.a: Replaced with ExecutorService fixed thread pool.
star.r: Bounded resource use. Handles load without crashing.

terms.what: Java thread has 6 states: NEW (created, not started), RUNNABLE (running or ready to run), BLOCKED (waiting for a lock), WAITING (waiting indefinitely), TIMED_WAITING (waiting with timeout), TERMINATED (finished).
terms.why: Understanding states helps diagnose thread issues — deadlocks show as BLOCKED, thread starvation shows as WAITING.
terms.how: Thread.getState() returns current state. Thread dumps (jstack) show all threads with their states. Most deadlocks are visible as circular BLOCKED chains.
terms.where: Thread monitoring, debugging, performance analysis. jstack, VisualVM, Actuator /threaddump endpoint in Spring Boot.
terms.when: Check thread states when: debugging deadlocks, diagnosing slow responses, profiling under load, investigating thread leaks.
terms.issue: Threads stuck in BLOCKED waiting for a lock held by another BLOCKED thread = deadlock. App freezes completely.
terms.fix: Take thread dump with jstack. Look for circular BLOCKED chains. Redesign locking strategy. Use tryLock() with timeout instead of synchronized to detect deadlocks gracefully.

star.s: Production app freezes periodically.
star.a: Thread dump shows Thread-A blocked on Lock-1 held by Thread-B, Thread-B blocked on Lock-2 held by Thread-A. Classic deadlock. Redesigned to always acquire locks in same order.

terms.what: Thread.sleep(ms) pauses the current thread for a fixed time — does NOT release any locks. wait() pauses and RELEASES the lock — other threads can enter the synchronized block.
terms.why: sleep() is for 'pause execution'. wait()/notify() is for thread coordination — 'wait until something happens'.
terms.how: wait() must be called inside synchronized block. notify() wakes one waiting thread. notifyAll() wakes all. Pattern: while(!condition) { wait(); } after notify sets condition.
terms.where: Producer-consumer patterns, custom coordination. Modern code uses higher-level tools: CountDownLatch, Semaphore, BlockingQueue instead.

terms.what: Runnable is run() — no return value, no checked exception. Callable is call() — returns a value and can throw checked exceptions. Both submitted to ExecutorService.
terms.why: Callable is more flexible — you get a result back from a background task via Future. Runnable is for fire-and-forget tasks.
terms.how: Callable<Integer> task = () -> heavyComputation(); Future<Integer> future = executor.submit(task); Integer result = future.get(); — blocks until done.
terms.where: Background computations returning results, async service calls, parallel data processing.
terms.when: Use Callable when you need the result. Use Runnable for fire-and-forget. Use CompletableFuture for chaining async operations.
terms.issue: future.get() blocks the calling thread until result is ready — defeats the purpose of async if you call it immediately.
terms.fix: Submit all tasks first, then call future.get() on all of them. Or use CompletableFuture.supplyAsync() for non-blocking chains.

star.s: Processing 100 API calls sequentially takes 30 seconds.
star.a: Submit all 100 as Callable tasks to thread pool. Collect all Futures. Call future.get() on each after all submitted.

subsections.title: Synchronization & Locks
subsections.keyRule: synchronized keyword uses intrinsic (monitor) lock — only one thread at a time. volatile ensures visibility across threads (no caching). ReentrantLock adds tryLock(), timeout, fairness. Atomic classes use lock-free CAS for simple counters. Rule: synchronize the minimum scope needed — over-synchronization kills performance.

terms.what: synchronized ensures only one thread executes a block or method at a time. Other threads wait (BLOCKED) until the current thread finishes and releases the lock.
terms.why: Without synchronization, multiple threads reading/writing shared data can produce wrong results — race condition.
terms.how: synchronized method: public synchronized void increment() { count++; }. synchronized block: synchronized(this) { count++; } — block is preferred, smaller scope.
terms.where: Any shared mutable state accessed by multiple threads: counters, caches, queues, session state.
terms.when: Use synchronized when threads share mutable data. Prefer the smallest possible synchronized scope to minimize contention.
terms.issue: Over-synchronizing (entire method when only 2 lines need protection) creates contention — threads wait unnecessarily, killing throughput.
terms.fix: Use synchronized block with minimum scope. Or use java.util.concurrent classes (AtomicInteger, ConcurrentHashMap) which are more efficient.

star.s: Thread-safe counter needed for hit tracking.
star.a: AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() — lock-free, faster than synchronized counter for simple increment.

terms.what: volatile guarantees that reads and writes to a variable are visible to all threads immediately — no CPU cache inconsistency. Does NOT make compound operations atomic.
terms.why: Without volatile, threads can cache variable values in CPU registers — one thread changes it, other threads still see old value.
terms.how: private volatile boolean running = true; — all threads always see the latest value. Does NOT help with running++ because that is two operations (read + write).
terms.where: Simple flags (running/stopped), singleton double-checked locking pattern, status variables read by multiple threads.
terms.when: Use volatile for: simple flags, variables written by one thread and read by many, the instance field in double-checked locking singleton.
terms.issue: volatile does NOT fix race conditions on compound operations. volatile int x; x++ is NOT atomic — still a race condition.
terms.fix: For compound operations use AtomicInteger, synchronized, or ReentrantLock. volatile only for simple read/write visibility.

star.s: Thread stop flag not working — thread ignores the flag change.
star.a: Added volatile to boolean stopRequested. Worker thread now sees the update immediately.
star.r: Thread stops cleanly on flag change.

terms.what: ReentrantLock is an explicit lock from java.util.concurrent.locks. More flexible than synchronized — supports tryLock(), lockInterruptibly(), fairness, and multiple Conditions.
terms.why: synchronized blocks cannot time out and cannot be interrupted while waiting. ReentrantLock solves these limitations.
terms.how: Lock lock = new ReentrantLock(); lock.lock(); try { ... } finally { lock.unlock(); } — ALWAYS unlock in finally. tryLock(timeout) returns false instead of blocking forever.
terms.where: Complex locking scenarios, deadlock prevention with timeout, fair queuing (new ReentrantLock(true)).
terms.when: Use ReentrantLock when synchronized is not enough: need timeout, interruptible lock, multiple condition variables, or lock fairness.
terms.issue: Forgetting to unlock in finally block = lock held forever = deadlock. Very common mistake.
terms.fix: ALWAYS: lock.lock(); try { work(); } finally { lock.unlock(); }. The finally guarantees unlock even on exceptions.

star.s: Deadlock between two services each holding a lock and waiting for the other.
star.a: Changed to tryLock(100ms). If tryLock fails, release own lock and retry.
star.r: Deadlock impossible — one side always backs off.

terms.what: Atomic classes provide thread-safe operations on single variables using CAS (Compare-And-Swap) — lock-free synchronization. Much faster than synchronized for simple operations.
terms.why: Synchronized for a simple counter is heavy — takes and releases a lock on every increment. AtomicInteger does it without any lock.
terms.how: AtomicInteger counter = new AtomicInteger(0); counter.incrementAndGet(); counter.compareAndSet(expected, update) — only updates if current value = expected. Lock-free.
terms.where: Counters, sequence generators, lock-free flags, optimistic locking patterns.
terms.when: Use Atomic classes when: you have a single variable needing thread-safe operations. Use synchronized/locks when you have multiple variables that must be updated together atomically.
terms.issue: AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() is atomic, but a sequence of: read, compute, set is NOT — use compareAndSet in a loop for that.
terms.fix: AtomicInteger supports updateAndGet(f) and accumulateAndGet(x, f) for complex atomic updates without loops.

star.s: Request counter using synchronized int.
star.a: Changed to AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet().
star.r: 5x faster for high-concurrency counters. No lock contention.

terms.what: Deadlock happens when two or more threads are each waiting for a lock held by the other — they wait forever. No thread can make progress. App freezes.
terms.why: Thread A holds Lock1 and needs Lock2. Thread B holds Lock2 and needs Lock1. Both wait forever. JVM has no automatic deadlock resolution.
terms.how: Classic: synchronized(lock1) { synchronized(lock2) {...} } in one thread, reversed order in another. If both threads run simultaneously, deadlock.
terms.where: Complex locking scenarios with multiple resources. Payment processing (lock account A, lock account B), database deadlocks.
terms.when: Deadlocks happen under concurrent load that you cannot always reproduce in tests. Requires careful lock ordering discipline.
terms.issue: Deadlocks can be rare and hard to reproduce — might only happen under specific timing conditions.
terms.fix: 1) Always acquire locks in the same order in all threads. 2) Use tryLock() with timeout. 3) Use higher-level abstractions (Database transactions, java.util.concurrent).

star.s: Transfer service deadlocks when two users transfer to each other simultaneously.
star.a: Normalize lock order — always lock account with smaller ID first.
star.r: No more deadlock regardless of transfer direction.

subsections.title: ExecutorService & Thread Pools
subsections.keyRule: Never create raw Thread per task in production — too expensive. Use ExecutorService thread pools. Types: FixedThreadPool (bounded, predictable), CachedThreadPool (unbounded — dangerous under load), SingleThreadExecutor (serialized), ScheduledThreadPool (periodic). Always shutdown() pool when done.

terms.what: ExecutorService manages a pool of worker threads and a queue of submitted tasks. You submit tasks — the pool executes them using existing threads.
terms.why: Thread creation is expensive (OS-level). Pool reuses threads. You also control the max thread count — prevent resource exhaustion.
terms.how: ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10); pool.submit(() -> doWork()); pool.shutdown(); pool.awaitTermination(60, SECONDS);
terms.where: All background and parallel work in production apps. Spring @Async uses it internally.
terms.when: Use ExecutorService instead of raw Thread everywhere. Size pool appropriately: CPU-bound tasks = #CPUs cores. IO-bound tasks = many more (depends on wait time).
terms.issue: Not shutting down ExecutorService = JVM keeps running even after main() ends — non-daemon threads keep JVM alive.
terms.fix: Always call pool.shutdown() in finally or use try-with-resources wrapper. In Spring, use @Bean lifecycle methods to shut down on context close.

star.s: JVM never exits after tests.
star.a: ExecutorService not shut down — threads keeping JVM alive. Added pool.shutdown() in @AfterAll.

terms.what: Future represents a result that will be available later. CompletableFuture (Java 8+) adds non-blocking chaining, combination, and error handling for async work.
terms.why: Future.get() blocks the calling thread — defeating async purpose. CompletableFuture lets you chain callbacks: 'when this finishes, do that' without blocking.
terms.how: CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchUser(id)).thenApply(user -> enrich(user)).thenAccept(user -> cache.put(user.id, user)).exceptionally(e -> handleError(e));
terms.where: Async REST calls, parallel data fetching, non-blocking pipelines in microservices.
terms.when: Use CompletableFuture for async chains. Use Future.get() only when you must wait for a result. Use allOf() to wait for multiple parallel tasks.

terms.what: Spring's @Async annotation makes a method run in a separate thread pool without you managing threads directly.
terms.why: Easy way to run slow operations (emails, notifications, reports) in the background without blocking the main request thread.
terms.how: @Async on a method + @EnableAsync on config class. Spring wraps the method call in an async executor. Returns void or CompletableFuture.
terms.where: Sending emails after registration, generating reports, pushing notifications, heavy background jobs.
terms.when: Use @Async for fire-and-forget background tasks. Return CompletableFuture<T> if you need to track the result.
terms.issue: @Async only works when called from outside the class — calling it from the same class bypasses the proxy and runs synchronously.
terms.fix: Always call @Async methods from a DIFFERENT bean. If you need self-call, inject the same bean and call through it.

star.s: Registration API takes 3 seconds — mostly sending welcome email.
star.a: Moved email sending to @Async method in EmailService. Registration API returns instantly.

subsections.title: ThreadLocal & Concurrent Collections
subsections.keyRule: ThreadLocal = private variable per thread — no sharing, no synchronization needed. ConcurrentHashMap = thread-safe map with bucket-level locking. CopyOnWriteArrayList = thread- safe list for read-heavy scenarios. BlockingQueue = thread-safe FIFO for producer-consumer.

terms.what: ThreadLocal stores a separate value for each thread. Thread A and Thread B each have their own copy — no sharing, no synchronization needed.
terms.why: Some data must be per-request or per-thread: current logged-in user, database connection, locale, transaction context.
terms.how: ThreadLocal<User> currentUser = new ThreadLocal<>(); currentUser.set(user); — stores for this thread. currentUser.get() — retrieves this thread's value. Other threads get null or their own value.
terms.where: Spring Security stores current user in SecurityContextHolder (backed by ThreadLocal). RequestContextHolder stores current HTTP request. MDC in logging stores per-request context.
terms.when: Use for request-scoped data that must be accessible deep in the call stack without passing as parameters everywhere.
terms.issue: Thread pools reuse threads. If you set a ThreadLocal and don't remove it, the next task on that thread sees the previous task's value — data leak.
terms.fix: ALWAYS call threadLocal.remove() when done — typically in a filter's finally block. Or use request-scoped Spring beans instead.

star.s: Security bug — user A's session data visible to user B.
star.a: ThreadLocal not removed after request. Thread pool reused thread. Next request saw old user. Added threadLocal.remove() in filter finally.

terms.what: BlockingQueue is a thread-safe FIFO queue where put() blocks if full and take() blocks if empty. Perfect for producer-consumer patterns.
terms.why: Producers and consumers can run at different speeds. BlockingQueue automatically buffers work and blocks producers/consumers to prevent overwhelming each other.
terms.how: BlockingQueue<Task> queue = new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(100); producer: queue.put(task); — blocks if full. consumer: Task t = queue.take(); — blocks if empty.
terms.where: Producer-consumer pipelines, work queues, event processing. ThreadPoolExecutor internally uses a BlockingQueue for its task queue.
terms.when: Use BlockingQueue for producer-consumer. Use ArrayBlockingQueue for bounded buffer. LinkedBlockingQueue for unbounded (careful — can OOM). SynchronousQueue for direct handoff.
terms.issue: Unbounded LinkedBlockingQueue can accept tasks faster than workers can process — queue grows until OOM.
terms.fix: Use ArrayBlockingQueue with a size limit. When full, caller blocks — provides natural back-pressure. Set queue size based on expected burst and processing speed.

star.s: File processing pipeline — reader much faster than processor.
star.a: ArrayBlockingQueue(1000) between reader and processor threads. Reader blocks when queue full — natural back-pressure.

terms.what: CopyOnWriteArrayList is a thread-safe List where every write (add, set, remove) creates a complete copy of the underlying array. Reads are lock-free.
terms.why: For read-heavy lists (read 1000x, write 1x), normal synchronization slows reads. CopyOnWrite makes reads free while writes pay the copy cost.
terms.how: Add: array copied → element added to copy → reference replaced atomically. Read: reads current snapshot — always consistent, no locks needed.
terms.where: Observer/listener lists (EventBus, Spring ApplicationListeners), config lists updated rarely but read constantly.
terms.when: Use CopyOnWriteArrayList when reads vastly outnumber writes. Do NOT use for write-heavy scenarios — copy on every write is expensive.
terms.issue: For write-heavy scenarios, copying on every write is extremely expensive — O(n) per write.
terms.fix: For balanced read/write, use ConcurrentLinkedQueue or synchronized ArrayList. CopyOnWrite is only for rare-write, frequent-read scenarios.

star.s: Event listener list causing ConcurrentModificationException when listeners are added during dispatch.
star.a: Changed to CopyOnWriteArrayList. Writers get a new copy; dispatching iterates the old snapshot.
star.r: No exception. Safe concurrent add + dispatch.

subsections.title: Thread Methods & Types
subsections.keyRule: yield() hints CPU to schedule another thread. join() waits for a thread to finish. interrupt() signals a thread to stop. Daemon threads die when all user threads finish. Thread priority is a HINT only — OS makes final decision.

terms.what: Thread.yield() is a hint to the JVM scheduler: 'I am willing to pause so another thread can run.' The scheduler may ignore it.
terms.why: For cooperative multitasking in test scenarios or simulations where you want to give other threads a chance to run.
terms.how: Thread.yield(); — current thread may be moved from RUNNING to RUNNABLE. No guarantee another thread actually runs.
terms.where: Rarely used in production code. Mostly seen in concurrency testing to increase interleaving. Loop-based spinlock implementations.

terms.what: thread.join() makes the current thread WAIT until the specified thread finishes execution. With timeout: thread.join(millis) — wait at most N milliseconds.
terms.why: When main thread needs all worker threads to complete before proceeding — batch job, parallel computation, graceful shutdown.
terms.how: Thread t1 = new Thread(task1); t1.start(); t1.join(); // main waits here until t1 done. Or: t1.join(5000); // wait at most 5 seconds.
terms.where: Test setup/teardown, batch processing that needs all parallel tasks done before results, graceful shutdown sequence.
terms.when: Use join() when you need to synchronize threads without a result. Use Future.get() or CompletableFuture.join() for tasks with results.
terms.issue: join() without timeout can block forever if the joined thread hangs.
terms.fix: Always use join(timeout). Check isAlive() after timeout to know if thread finished or timed out.

star.s: Main thread exits before worker threads finish — partial results.
star.a: Added t.join() for each worker thread before processing results.
star.r: Main waits for all workers. Complete results.

terms.what: thread.interrupt() sets the interrupt flag on a thread. The thread should check isInterrupted() or handle InterruptedException to stop cleanly.
terms.why: Threads cannot be forcibly killed safely. Interruption is a cooperative signal: 'please stop when convenient'.
terms.how: Worker: while(!Thread.currentThread().isInterrupted()) { doWork(); } — checks flag. Or: catch(InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); break; }
terms.where: Thread pool shutdown, task cancellation, responsive thread termination, stopping background services.
terms.when: Use interrupt() to signal threads to stop. ALWAYS re-interrupt or handle InterruptedException properly.
terms.issue: Swallowing InterruptedException (catch and ignore) loses the signal — thread never stops, causes hangs on shutdown.
terms.fix: In catch(InterruptedException e): either propagate it (throw or rethrow), or restore the flag: Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); then break/return.

star.s: Application takes 30 seconds to shutdown — background threads don't stop.
star.a: Added interrupt check in worker loops. Shutdown calls interrupt() on all threads.

terms.what: Daemon thread is a background thread that dies automatically when all user (non- daemon) threads exit. JVM exits without waiting for daemon threads to finish.
terms.why: Background maintenance tasks (GC, monitoring, heartbeats) should not prevent JVM from exiting when the app is done.
terms.how: Thread t = new Thread(task); t.setDaemon(true); t.start(); — must set BEFORE start(). GC thread, finalizer thread are daemon threads.
terms.where: Background monitoring, log flushing, heartbeat sender, JVM internal threads.
terms.when: Use daemon threads for background infrastructure tasks that should not hold JVM alive. Do NOT use for work that must complete — it may be killed.
terms.issue: Daemon thread killed mid-write when JVM exits — data loss or corruption if it was doing file/DB writes.

terms.what: Java threads have priority 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX), default 5 (NORM). Higher priority threads are more likely to be scheduled but it is a HINT, not a guarantee.
terms.why: Hint the OS scheduler to favor certain threads — useful for latency-sensitive tasks.
terms.how: thread.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY); — 10. Thread.MIN_PRIORITY = 1, Thread.NORM_PRIORITY = 5. Platform-specific: on Linux, priorities map to nice values.
terms.where: Real-time-ish applications, latency-sensitive threads vs background processing.
terms.when: Rarely rely on priority for correctness — it is non-deterministic. Use proper concurrency structures instead.
terms.issue: Priority inversion: low-priority thread holds a lock needed by high-priority thread. High-priority thread starved.
terms.fix: Design to avoid priority inversion. Use lock-free data structures or priority-aware mutexes in critical sections.

star.s: Interview: can you guarantee a high-priority thread always runs first?
star.a: No — thread priority is a scheduling HINT, not a guarantee. OS has final say. Priority inversion can cause opposite behavior.

subsections.title: Java Memory Model & Advanced Locks
subsections.keyRule: Java Memory Model: happens-before rule guarantees visibility. volatile ensures visibility. synchronized ensures both visibility and atomicity. Instruction reordering by JIT/CPU is real — happens-before prevents incorrect reordering. ReadWriteLock allows concurrent reads but exclusive writes.

terms.what: Happens-before is Java's formal guarantee: if action A happens-before action B, then everything A wrote is visible to B. Without it, threads can see stale data.
terms.why: CPUs and JIT compilers reorder instructions for performance. Happens-before rules define when reordering is NOT allowed.
terms.how: Rules: 1) Monitor unlock happens-before lock by another thread. 2) volatile write happens-before volatile read. 3) Thread.start() happens-before thread's actions. 4) Thread.join() happens-before caller's next action.
terms.where: Every synchronization point: synchronized blocks, volatile variables, thread start/join, object publication.
terms.when: Think about happens-before when: sharing data between threads, checking if a shared variable update is visible, designing lock-free data structures.
terms.issue: No happens-before = thread B may see the object in an uninitialized or partially- initialized state even after A 'finished' writing it.
terms.fix: Use synchronized, volatile, or java.util.concurrent structures — they all establish happens-before guarantees.

star.s: Interview: how does Java ensure one thread sees another's writes?
star.a: Java Memory Model defines happens-before. synchronized unlock and volatile write create visibility guarantees. Without them, CPU caches can give stale values.

terms.what: JIT compiler and CPU can reorder instructions for performance as long as the result is the same within a single thread. But this can break multi-threaded code.
terms.why: Reordering speeds up single-threaded execution. But shared data written by one thread may appear in a different order to another thread without synchronization.

terms.what: Livelock: two or more threads keep responding to each other, both 'active' (not BLOCKED) but making no progress. Unlike deadlock, threads are not stuck — they keep working but cancel each other out.
terms.why: Classic livelock: two threads each try to be 'polite' — if they can't get both locks, they release their own lock and retry. Both keep releasing and retrying forever.
terms.how: ThreadA: while(!tryLock(lock1,lock2)) { release(lock1); sleep(random); }. ThreadB: same but acquires lock2 first. Both keep backing off simultaneously.
terms.where: Retry logic that reacts to the other party's actions. Message retry systems, politeness protocols.
terms.when: Detect livelock via: CPU is high but no progress made, both threads in RUNNABLE but doing nothing useful.
terms.issue: Livelock is harder to debug than deadlock — threads are not blocked, just uselessly busy.
terms.fix: Add randomized backoff (random sleep duration). Or use a time-limited retry with escalation. Avoid symmetric retry logic.

star.s: Two services both retry on conflict — system looks busy but never processes requests.
star.a: Livelock from symmetric retry. Added jitter to retry delay.
star.r: One side retries slightly later, other succeeds. Progress made.

terms.what: Starvation: a thread is perpetually denied CPU time because higher-priority threads always run. The starved thread never (or rarely) gets to execute.
terms.why: Thread priority systems and non-fair locks can starve lower-priority threads if high- priority ones dominate.
terms.how: With ReentrantLock(true) — fair lock — threads get CPU in arrival order, preventing starvation. Without fairness, a busy thread group can starve others.
terms.where: Thread pools with mix of priority levels. Non-fair locks under heavy contention. Reactive systems with unconstrained thread pools.
terms.when: Diagnose starvation: thread in RUNNABLE but never actually makes progress. Check thread contention in JProfiler/VisualVM.
terms.issue: Reader threads can starve writer threads with ReadWriteLock — if readers always present, writer waits forever.
terms.fix: Use fair variants: new ReentrantLock(true) or StampedLock. Limit thread pool size. Ensure all thread types get scheduled.

star.s: Low-priority data sync task never runs despite being in 'running' pool.
star.a: High-priority API tasks always available — sync task starved. Set fair thread pool. Added dedicated fixed-size pool for sync.

terms.what: ReadWriteLock allows multiple concurrent readers OR one exclusive writer — not both. Much better than synchronized for read-heavy data.
terms.why: synchronized allows only one thread at a time — even readers block each other. ReadWriteLock lets thousands of readers run in parallel.

terms.what: StampedLock is a high-performance lock with three modes: write, read, and optimistic read. Optimistic read allows reading without a lock — if data unchanged, no lock needed.
terms.why: ReadWriteLock read locks still involve lock acquisition overhead. Optimistic reads in StampedLock skip locking entirely for even better performance.
terms.how: long stamp = sl.tryOptimisticRead(); readData(); if (!sl.validate(stamp)) { stamp = sl.readLock(); try { readData(); } finally { sl.unlock(stamp); } }
terms.where: High-performance read-heavy caches, concurrent coordinate systems, any read- heavy shared state needing maximum throughput.
terms.when: Use StampedLock for maximum read performance when data rarely changes and you can validate the optimistic read.
terms.issue: StampedLock is NOT reentrant — a thread that already holds the lock cannot acquire it again without deadlock.
terms.fix: Use with care. Never use StampedLock from recursive methods. For reentrant lock with read/write, use ReentrantReadWriteLock.

star.s: Config cache with ReadWriteLock still shows lock contention under 1000 concurrent readers.
star.a: Changed to StampedLock with optimistic reads.
star.r: Near-zero lock overhead for reads. Throughput doubles.

subsections.title: ThreadPoolExecutor Config
subsections.keyRule: ThreadPoolExecutor has: corePoolSize (min threads kept alive), maximumPoolSize (max threads), keepAliveTime (idle thread timeout), work queue (task buffer), RejectedExecutionHandler (what to do when full). Understanding these prevents thread explosion and task loss.

terms.what: ThreadPoolExecutor is the underlying class behind all Executors factory methods. Gives full control over pool size, queue type, thread lifecycle, and rejection policy.
terms.why: Executors.newFixedThreadPool() hides settings. With ThreadPoolExecutor you tune every aspect for your workload.
terms.how: new ThreadPoolExecutor(coreSize, maxSize, keepAlive, TimeUnit, new ArrayBlockingQueue<>(1000), new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy());
terms.where: Production thread pools that need tuning. High-throughput batch processors. Microservices with mixed CPU/IO tasks.
terms.when: Use ThreadPoolExecutor when you need control over rejection policy, queue bound, or dynamic pool sizing. Executors.newFixed/CachedThreadPool are OK for simple cases.
terms.issue: Executors.newCachedThreadPool() = unbounded threads. Under burst, thousands of threads created — OOM or OS limit hit.

terms.what: When ThreadPoolExecutor queue is full and max threads reached, new tasks are rejected. Four built-in policies: AbortPolicy (exception), CallerRunsPolicy (caller executes), DiscardPolicy (silently drop), DiscardOldestPolicy (drop oldest).
terms.why: Default AbortPolicy throws RejectedExecutionException. Choose policy based on what is acceptable when the system is overloaded.
terms.how: CallerRunsPolicy: submitter's thread executes the task — slows down the caller, provides natural back-pressure. AbortPolicy: throws exception — caller must handle. DiscardPolicy: silently drops — risk of data loss.
terms.where: High-load production services. Batch processors. Background job pools.
terms.when: CallerRunsPolicy = best for back-pressure without data loss. AbortPolicy = caller decides. DiscardPolicy = only for non-critical tasks.
terms.issue: DiscardPolicy silently drops tasks — could cause data loss in critical workflows without any warning.
terms.fix: Use CallerRunsPolicy for important tasks — it never drops and naturally slows the producer. Log rejections regardless of policy.

star.s: Background email queue drops tasks silently under load.
star.a: Changed to CallerRunsPolicy + added rejection logging.
star.r: Tasks never dropped. System slows under load but all emails sent.

terms.what: shutdown() stops accepting new tasks but finishes existing ones. shutdownNow() tries to stop immediately (interrupts running tasks). awaitTermination() blocks until all tasks finish or timeout.
terms.why: Clean pool shutdown is critical — prevents data loss from abruptly killed tasks and prevents thread leaks from non-terminated pools.
terms.how: pool.shutdown(); pool.awaitTermination(60, TimeUnit.SECONDS); if(!pool.isTerminated()) { pool.shutdownNow(); }
terms.where: Application shutdown hooks, test teardown, graceful service shutdown.
terms.when: Always use shutdown() + awaitTermination(). Only use shutdownNow() as last resort — it may interrupt tasks mid-operation.
terms.issue: Not calling shutdown() = thread pool keeps JVM alive after work is done. Also = threads leaked in containers.
terms.fix: Register shutdown hook or @PreDestroy in Spring: pool.shutdown() in cleanup method.

star.s: Integration tests hang — JVM never exits.
star.a: ExecutorService not shut down after tests. Added pool.shutdown() in @AfterAll.
star.r: Tests complete and JVM exits cleanly.

terms.what: LongAdder is a high-concurrency counter that internally uses multiple cells to reduce contention. Much faster than AtomicLong under heavy concurrent increments.
terms.why: AtomicLong uses CAS — under high contention, many threads retry repeatedly. LongAdder splits the counter into multiple cells, threads increment different cells, sum() combines them.
terms.how: LongAdder counter = new LongAdder(); counter.increment(); counter.add(5); long total = counter.sum(); — sum() adds all cells. TERM MEANING
terms.where: High-frequency event counters, hit counters, throughput meters. Micrometer/Prometheus counters use LongAdder internally.
terms.when: Use LongAdder instead of AtomicLong for high-concurrency counters where exact real-time value is not critical. AtomicLong is better when you need CAS semantics.
terms.issue: sum() gives an approximate value under concurrent updates — it is not a snapshot. By the time you read, other threads may have incremented.
terms.fix: For exact counts at a moment, use synchronization. For high-speed approximate counters, LongAdder is perfect.

star.s: AtomicLong counter bottleneck under 100 concurrent threads — CAS contention.
star.a: Replaced with LongAdder.

terms.what: ABA problem: CAS checks if value is still A. Thread reads A, another changes to B, then back to A. First thread CAS succeeds even though the value changed (and changed back).
terms.why: For some algorithms, even if the value looks the same, the fact it was changed matters. Linked list node reuse can cause corruption.
terms.how: Thread 1 reads head = Node(A). Thread 2 removes A, adds B, adds A back. Thread 1's CAS(head, A, C) succeeds — but the list structure changed!
terms.where: Lock-free linked lists, lock-free stacks, any CAS-based algorithm where identity matters, not just value.
terms.when: Worry about ABA in lock-free algorithms using CAS on mutable objects. For simple counters, ABA does not matter.
terms.issue: CAS-based lock-free stack: push then pop then push same node — ABA lets old CAS succeed corrupting the stack.
terms.fix: Use AtomicStampedReference — adds a version stamp. CAS succeeds only if both value AND stamp match. Each change increments the stamp.

star.s: Interview: what is the ABA problem?
star.a: CAS sees value A, another thread changes to B and back to A, first thread's CAS incorrectly succeeds. Solution: AtomicStampedReference with version counter.

terms.what: AtomicStampedReference wraps a reference plus an integer stamp. CAS succeeds only when BOTH the reference AND the stamp match. Solves the ABA problem.
terms.why: Each update increments the stamp, so A→B→A has stamp 0→1→2. CAS with stamp 0 fails because stamp is now 2.
terms.how: AtomicStampedReference<Node> ref = new AtomicStampedReference<>(head, 0); int[] stamp = {0}; Node current = ref.get(stamp); ref.compareAndSet(current, newNode, stamp[0], stamp[0]+1);
terms.where: Lock-free algorithms that need ABA protection, lock-free collections, high- performance concurrent data structures.
terms.when: Use when implementing lock-free algorithms where identity changes matter, not just value changes.
terms.issue: AtomicStampedReference has higher overhead than AtomicReference — two values to compare.
terms.fix: Only use when ABA is a real concern. For counters, AtomicInteger/LongAdder are sufficient.

star.s: Lock-free stack shows rare data corruption under high concurrency.
star.a: ABA problem — nodes reused without stamp. Changed to AtomicStampedReference.
star.r: Corruption eliminated. Stack correct under all load levels.

subsections.title: java.util.concurrent Utilities
subsections.keyRule: CountDownLatch: one-time gate — wait for N events. CyclicBarrier: reusable barrier — wait for N threads to reach same point. Semaphore: control concurrency slots. Exchanger: swap objects between two threads. Phaser: flexible multi-phase barrier.

terms.what: CountDownLatch lets one or more threads wait until a set of operations is complete. Initialized with a count. Each countDown() decrements. await() blocks until count reaches 0.
terms.why: Wait for N parallel tasks to all complete before proceeding. Replaces 'start all threads then join all'.
terms.how: CountDownLatch latch = new CountDownLatch(5); for(int i=0; i<5; i++) { executor.submit(() -> { doWork(); latch.countDown(); }); } latch.await(); // blocks until all 5 done.
terms.where: Parallel test setup, parallel data loading, starting multiple services, integration test synchronization.
terms.when: Use CountDownLatch for one-time wait scenarios. Cannot be reset — use CyclicBarrier for reusable barriers.
terms.issue: If one task throws and never calls countDown(), latch.await() blocks forever. Always call countDown() in finally.
terms.fix: Use latch.await(timeout, TimeUnit) and check return value. Always countDown() in finally: try { work(); } finally { latch.countDown(); }

star.s: Test needs all 10 DB partitions loaded before assertions.
star.a: CountDownLatch(10). Each loader thread calls countDown() when done. Test calls await().
star.r: Test only proceeds when all partitions ready.

terms.what: CyclicBarrier makes N threads wait for each other at a common barrier point. When all N threads arrive, they all proceed — and the barrier resets for reuse.
terms.why: For parallel algorithms where all threads must complete one phase before starting the next — e.g., parallel matrix multiplication phases.
terms.how: CyclicBarrier barrier = new CyclicBarrier(3); // 3 parties. Each thread: doPhaseWork(); barrier.await(); // waits until all 3 arrive. Then: doPhaseWork2(); barrier.await();
terms.where: Parallel simulations, multi-phase parallel algorithms, game round synchronization.
terms.when: Use CyclicBarrier when threads must repeatedly synchronize at phases. Use CountDownLatch for one-time coordination.
terms.issue: If one thread throws BrokenBarrierException (another thread failed/interrupted), all other waiting threads also get BrokenBarrierException.
terms.fix: Handle BrokenBarrierException. Recreate barrier if needed. The CyclicBarrier can be reset() but it is risky under concurrent use.

star.s: Parallel simulation: all agents must complete each time step before proceeding.
star.a: CyclicBarrier(agentCount). Each agent calls await() at end of time step.
star.r: All agents perfectly synchronized per step.

terms.what: Semaphore controls concurrent access to a resource pool. Has N permits. acquire() decrements permits (blocks if 0). release() increments permits. Like a parking lot with N spaces.
terms.why: Limit concurrency — only N requests can access a database, API, or file simultaneously.
terms.how: Semaphore sem = new Semaphore(10); // 10 concurrent allowed. sem.acquire(); try { accessResource(); } finally { sem.release(); }
terms.where: Connection pool size limiter, API rate limiting, throttling parallel downloads, limiting concurrent writes to disk.
terms.when: Use Semaphore when you want to limit concurrent access to a shared resource to a maximum number.
terms.issue: Not releasing in finally — permits lost permanently. Semaphore drains to 0 — all threads block forever. TERM MEANING
terms.fix: ALWAYS: sem.acquire(); try { work(); } finally { sem.release(); } — release in finally is mandatory.

star.s: DB getting 500 concurrent connections — crashes.
star.a: Semaphore(50) before DB calls. Only 50 concurrent DB operations. Others queue.

terms.what: Exchanger lets exactly two threads swap objects. Both threads call exchange(obj). Each blocks until the other calls exchange, then they swap and both continue.
terms.why: Useful for pipeline designs where two threads produce and consume from each other, taking turns.
terms.how: Exchanger<DataBuffer> ex = new Exchanger<>(); // Thread A: filled = ex.exchange(empty); // Thread B: empty = ex.exchange(filled); — they swap buffers.
terms.where: Double-buffered pipelines, producer-consumer with buffer reuse, concurrent data exchange protocols.
terms.when: Use when exactly two threads need to coordinate a swap. More complex scenarios need other mechanisms.
terms.issue: If one thread does not call exchange(), the other blocks forever.
terms.fix: Use exchange(obj, timeout, unit) with timeout to avoid indefinite blocking.

star.s: Interview: how do two threads efficiently swap data?
star.a: Exchanger — each thread calls exchange() with their data. Both block until other arrives, then swap.

terms.what: Phaser is a flexible, reusable barrier that supports dynamic party registration and multi-phase coordination. More powerful than CyclicBarrier and CountDownLatch.
terms.why: CyclicBarrier has fixed party count. Phaser lets threads register/deregister dynamically during execution.
terms.how: Phaser phaser = new Phaser(1); // 1 registered (main). phaser.register(); // add a party. phaser.arriveAndAwaitAdvance(); // barrier. phaser.arriveAndDeregister(); // leave.
terms.where: Complex parallel algorithms with variable number of participants, multi-phase iteration, fork/join style coordination.
terms.when: Use Phaser when party count changes dynamically. Otherwise CyclicBarrier is simpler.
terms.issue: Phaser is complex — easy to miscount registrations. Unregistered party never triggers phase advance.
terms.fix: Track registrations carefully. Use phaser.getRegisteredParties() for debugging.

star.s: Parallel crawler adds/removes workers dynamically.
star.a: Phaser — workers register on start, deregister on completion. Phase advances when all active workers done.

terms.what: ConcurrentLinkedQueue is an unbounded, non-blocking, thread-safe queue using CAS operations. Lock-free — high throughput for concurrent access.
terms.why: BlockingQueue blocks producers and consumers. ConcurrentLinkedQueue never blocks — poll() returns null if empty, offer() always succeeds.
terms.how: ConcurrentLinkedQueue<Task> q = new ConcurrentLinkedQueue<>(); q.offer(task); Task t = q.poll(); // null if empty — non-blocking.
terms.where: High-throughput message passing between threads, work-stealing queues, event queues in reactive systems.
terms.when: Use when non-blocking poll behavior is needed and tasks can be polled repeatedly. Use BlockingQueue for producer-consumer with blocking semantics.
terms.issue: Unbounded — can grow without limit and OOM under heavy load if consumers are slow.
terms.fix: Add capacity check before offering, or use a semaphore to limit total items in the queue. TERM MEANING

star.s: High-frequency event system using synchronized queue — contention.
star.a: ConcurrentLinkedQueue — lock-free CAS operations.

terms.what: ConcurrentSkipListMap is a concurrent sorted map with expected O(log n) operations. Thread-safe without global locks. Iterators are weakly consistent.
terms.why: TreeMap needs external synchronization — global lock kills concurrency. ConcurrentSkipListMap gives concurrent sorted access.
terms.how: ConcurrentSkipListMap<Integer,String> map = new ConcurrentSkipListMap<>(); map.put(5,'five'); map.firstKey() → smallest. map.subMap(1,10) → sorted range.
terms.where: Concurrent sorted indexes, time-series event maps, leaderboards updated by multiple threads.
terms.when: Use ConcurrentSkipListMap when sorted order AND concurrent access are both needed.
terms.issue: ConcurrentSkipListMap is slower than ConcurrentHashMap for unsorted access — O(log n) vs O(1).
terms.fix: Use ConcurrentHashMap for unordered concurrent maps. ConcurrentSkipListMap only when sort order matters.

star.s: Concurrent leaderboard needs sorted scores accessed by multiple threads.
star.a: ConcurrentSkipListMap<Score,Player>. Concurrent updates and sorted iteration.
star.r: Thread-safe sorted leaderboard without global lock.

terms.what: ThreadLocal.withInitial(Supplier) creates a ThreadLocal with a default value factory. On first get(), each thread gets a freshly created value from the supplier.
terms.why: Without withInitial(), first call returns null — you must check for null every time. withInitial() removes null checks.
terms.how: ThreadLocal<SimpleDateFormat> formatter = ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new SimpleDateFormat('yyyy-MM-dd')); formatter.get().format(date); — each thread gets its own formatter.
terms.where: Per-thread formatters (SimpleDateFormat is not thread-safe), per-thread random generators, per-thread DB connections.
terms.when: Use withInitial() for any ThreadLocal that needs a non-null default — almost always.
terms.issue: SimpleDateFormat shared across threads = data corruption. withInitial() ensures each thread has its own instance.
terms.fix: ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new SimpleDateFormat(...)) — each thread gets its own instance. Modern code: use DateTimeFormatter (thread-safe) instead.

star.s: NullPointerException on formatter.get() in worker threads.
star.a: Changed to ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new SimpleDateFormat(pattern)).
star.r: Each thread auto-gets a formatter on first use.

terms.what: InheritableThreadLocal allows child threads to inherit the parent thread's ThreadLocal values. Regular ThreadLocal: child threads get null. InheritableThreadLocal: child gets a copy of parent's value.
terms.why: When spawning child threads that need the context of the parent — user context, trace ID, locale.
terms.how: InheritableThreadLocal<String> ctx = new InheritableThreadLocal<>(); ctx.set('user-123'); // parent. new Thread(() -> { ctx.get(); // 'user-123' — inherited from parent! }).start();
terms.where: Request context propagation into async threads, trace ID inheritance, security context propagation. TERM MEANING
terms.when: Use when background threads spawned from request threads need the parent's context. Be careful with thread pools — inherited value is from the thread creation time, not latest.
terms.issue: Thread pool reuses threads — InheritableThreadLocal value inherited when thread was first created, not when task was submitted. Child gets wrong context.
terms.fix: Use TransmittableThreadLocal (TTL library by Alibaba) for thread pool-safe context propagation. Spring's RequestContextHolder handles this for Spring MVC.

star.s: Trace ID not visible in @Async background threads.
star.a: Changed to InheritableThreadLocal for trace ID. For thread pools, use Spring's TaskDecorator to copy context on submit.
